So this third habit is just to keep it

that
it's just as important in the AI world

And so this means that you should
establish simplicity guardrails to

tight deadlines, there's roadmap pivots,

So installing these guard rails will

So in this research paper that we

um we actually saw that most the pop

Now again this is a rule that you were

One that I think about from just like my
experience of doing refactors and
changing things in the codebase is
sometimes you're you write a new

So there's like that kind of just

should no longer be used. You wouldn't

But the second reason which is much more

like you don't realize it's not being

Now you may think okay that's fine
that's low risk wouldn't actually happen

And this is where Sonar can proactively

Okay, let's [clears throat] talk about

fullText: Welcome everybody and thank you for joining us early. We'll get started in just a few seconds. Okay. Uh the session will be recorded and we'll send out a link via email in the coming days. And a quick heads up that we will include a poll during today's webinar. And please do participate. The more that do, the more interesting the results. And finally, we'll pop up a brief survey at the very end. We appreciate your feedback. Now, I'm pleased to introduce our presenter today, Erin. And as I mentioned earlier, uh Erin leads the code orchestration domain here at Sonar and her team focuses on a critical mission, building the platforms and tools that help developers improve code quality and security. And so, just to give a quick rundown of where we're going to go is first, we are going to start talking about the AI coding transformation and what we've seen over the past six months, year, etc. And then how do we navigate these challenges and risks which will lead to how the seven habits that you as an engineer, engineering manager, director, security personnel, DevOps engineer, whatever your title may be can habits you can adopt to be effective with how you interact with these AI agents. We'll wrap up with the path forward and our final screen is three QR codes. So have your phones ready to look at some resources that I'm going to share. So to start over the past few years, I mean I think you everyone is aware especially over the past six months of this shift that we have seen in software development and it's completely transformed the way that we work. Roughly 84% of software developers are using AI coding tools and that number is expected to grow rapidly not only in the number of developers that are using tools but also the frequency of their use. A recent article mentioned that cursor writes almost 1 billion lines of accepted code a day. So to put that in context for you, prior to the AI coding world, humans only wrote like two to three billion lines of code per day. So this is a staggering number and this isn't what cursor writes, it's what's accepted. So you know that what cursor writes is even more than that. And that's a staggering number. And finally, a report from Gartner estimates that 90% of engineers will be using AI coding assistance by 2028. And I would not be surprised if we beat that metric with how quickly adoption is happening. And so that AI is about to write explosive volume of code. And so what is it about AI coding that has so many people jump on board so quickly? Well, first it increases your developers productivity and speed. And so simply writing code is faster. And I think many of us have seen that. Next, there's now a democratization of coding. It's much more accessible to beginners. You don't necessarily have to have a CS degree, a master's degree, boot camps, etc. to start writing code. I know people who've written apps and they haven't even touched one line of code. And so, it's much more accessible to many more people. And finally, it enhances your developer experience because your developers can spend more time focusing on the creative problem solving and strategic thinking as opposed to the monotony of always just writing code. So, this sounds really great, but as always, there's a catch. The developers currently have very low trust in the code being written. Um, I mean, I've know you guys probably have. I have seen multiple mistakes from hallucinations where something as simple as the naming conventions are incorrect and they rename something in or they import non-existent libraries and so it's not the AI agents have a long way to go before we can truly trust just some of the basic outputs they have. But then additionally the code isn't nearly as performant. we see that there's inefficiencies and complexities in code that even my junior developers wouldn't add. And so this makes writing sophisticated code with AI that much more difficult. Yes, you'll get code out the door faster, but if you want to have good scrutiny and like long-term code that you don't have to deal with later and write it well, you're going to have to rewrite it or restructure it. um especially if you're using AI for these big chunks of code like a net new application as opposed to smaller fixes and updates. And finally, we've seen a number of security concerns. So at Sonar, we actually recently published a research paper written by a few of our principal engineers here that analyzed five of the most popular LLMs to see how they wrote code and analyze the quality. So this paper confirms just what these people are seeing that throughout the presentation I'll reference it and give you examples and we'll dive deeper into it at the end I will give a reference as well to this paper so that you can dig in yourself and see all the issues that there are with current LMS and so the fact that a ton of code is being generated is great but developers have low trust and so that is what has led to what we call the engineering productivity paradox. So if so much more code is being produced this quickly, why is our productivity only increasing by an incremental amount? At Google, they reported that over 30% of their code is written by AI, but they're only seeing a 10% lift in productivity. And this diagram here more or less illustrates that point. You have the blue line that is the steadying increase in your AI generated code. How quickly code is getting written. However, the red line is the pace at which human engineers are able to review and verify the code for quality, security and maintainability. And often times the red line cannot keep up with the blue line. So this puts your organization in a pickle. Do we move fast by shipping things with minimal vetting and just get code out the door, get that new feature out there, even if there's bugs and security issues, it's fine. Let's just move. Or do you stay along the red line and you make sure that everything is secure and safe, it's vetted, it's well written. What decision, which direction you going to go? And so that's what we're going to dive into today. We're going to talk about how sonar specifically or even just some habits outside of sonar can help you safely move closer to the blue line and increase your productivity while still keeping your code maintainable and secure. And this is what led to the seven habits of highly effective AI coding. Now this originally came in the form of a blog from our CEO Tar Shakott back in I think like April or May when he was seeing this big shift that was happening in the software development world and started thinking what can we do to stay ahead of the curve here. So let's start. Number one developers are accountable. So, you're going to hear me say this a few times throughout that the same rules that you were taught as an engineer before the AI world still apply to the AI coding world. But if you're a vibe coder and you don't have a formal engineering background, you may not know what some of these rules are. So, this one here is all about if you write or approve, that's always the biggest one. Write or approve your code, you are responsible for it. So this means that you should never blindly accept your AI suggestions, but instead scrutinize them as you would any other code. You see, AI agents, they may produce good code, as we kind of talked about earlier, that could pass the unit tests, but often times this code has security gaps or performance issues. Uh a very simple example that I think about is if I just ask an AI assistant to process a list of user ids uh from the database and I look at the unit tests and great I can see that it's taking this list of ids. It's returning the results. Perfect. I test things locally. Works great. Okay. I don't need to look at the code. I can see from the unit tests and my testing that we're good. However, when you get to production, you have a big performance bottleneck. Why is that? Well, what if the AI happened to just write a for loop for every item hit the database? And now, when you're processing tens of millions of items, you're making that many calls to the database where your AI agent should have batched it or made parallel calls. That's a very simple example and most likely I agents will not be making that kind of mistake but that's the kind of thing that you can see is you something that your junior developer wouldn't do that you wouldn't have that much scrutiny for in a PR your AI agent may do and you run into these performance issues and so for this reason your developers need to review and validate and approve everything the AI agent writes and as I said earlier cursor is accepting ing 1 billion lines of code a day. So that's a lot of code. It's swast new code for your developers to look at which is exhausting and they also have to change their mindset to be security focused as well. And so in order to help developers with this daunting task, this is where Sonar can provide features like a PR analysis tool that will do things like detect AI code. We can measure code coverage. we can detect your code spells and provide you guard rails against these kind of bugs. And this is a nice tool to have to the side of the engineer so they know that some of the baseline items are are already covered by sonar so they can focus on the more challenging or higher level thinking and they can trust sonar um to do that scrutiny for them. So the next habit is to overdo your project context. So once again, always a good practice to document your code. But I'll be honest, I will speak for myself here. I know some devs are great at it, but most developers are not great at documentation. Um, and I will be the first to raise my hand in that camp. And so in general like you want to have your clear comprehensive project documentation where you outline the project's architecture. You want to make sure you have lists of the intentions and help your developers understand whether the new code they're writing is fitting into the over overall architecture and talk through any nuances you may have to the codebase. Now when we think about documentation there's two main areas. You have the macro context which is your architecture and you have the micro context which is your AI prompt. Now when I think about how I want to interact with AI agents, I try to put myself in the shoes of myself as an engineer prior to the AI world. So let's say you have a new hire that joins your team and as a part of the onboarding you're like hey this is a codebase that we own. Go learn about it. Currently we have like internal wikis that have our architecture decisions written there. The diagram how everything fits. Then we have a readme in our repository that tells you how to spin up the code, run it locally, test it, execute it, etc. But then there's always a lot of context that comes just from the side conversations, questions that my new engineer is asking, right? and they're asking me questions this side and I'm answering them. And this is the kind of detail that the AI agent actually misses from the macro context perspective and you need to put into your code so they can see it. So this means adding architecture design decisions like your ADRs into your code and documenting not just what the design is but why you made that decision to architect things that way. This means your readme should be really robust and any of the side conversation items that you'd have with a new hire, you want to have within your repository because that gives your AI agent the context they need to make the right decisions when it comes to architecture, architectural thoughts of how to implement things. And then you go to the micro context and I always view that that's like the AI prompt, but I view it like a Jira ticket. So at Sonar whenever we're implementing a new task or a new item we have a jury ticket and best practices is to list out in your jur ticket the what, how, why, put some requirements in there, functional, non-functional, edge cases, etc. And that's what your engineer takes to then start implementing their project. But when you write to AI right now, I'm going to guess many of you just say something very brief such as create a new function that takes in these parameters and outputs a boolean and that's the amount of context you give it. You don't tell it of the edge cases and the functional non-functional requirements etc. And that's the kind of micro context that will help you end up with better code. And yes, it means doing more work up front and that's exhausting. However, it means you have less rework that you have to do later and your code is more accurate. You're going to have less bugs. And so, think about AI like a teammate. Um, and put all the communication you can in the project repository and in the prompt that you're writing. And remember that documentation is code. So this third habit is just to keep it simple. Like that goes without saying everyone likes simple code. What I think people often miss though is the fact that it's just as important in the AI world as it is for human developers. Our AI tools actually struggle to analyze, refactor, or debug code with high cognitive complexity because the large context windows and the complex control flows actually degrade the model's ability to reason effectively. And so this means that you should establish simplicity guardrails to prevent the kind of spaghetti code that we are all used to seeing when there's tight deadlines, there's roadmap pivots, you do quick fixes, whatever it means. So installing these guard rails will help prevent your AI agents from writing complex code. So in this research paper that we released that I mentioned earlier [clears throat] um we actually saw that most the pop some of the most popular LLMs wrote extremely high cognitive and cyclatic complexity code. Now you may look at that and be like okay cool they write complex code that's not a big deal my code is functioning great right now it does not take that long to add anything new with an AI agent. Well, my question to you is what percentage of your code base is actually written by an AI agent? If you're an existing company and you just started using agents, that's maybe at 10% 20%. So, you're fine for now. But what happens 6 months, one year from now, your code is probably 50 75% written by an agent. Now, at that point, that's going to cause problems because if AI agents still a year from now struggle to understand complex code, then it's going to make it a lot harder for them to start adding updates to your system without just adding more and more code. And so, this is the kind of thing that you want to be aware of up front and start acting on now so you don't get in this situation 6 months to a year from now. At Sonar, we say that quality is equal to security. So in order to help keep your code quality high, put rules in place. You can add rules like each function has to be less than 100 lines of code. Let's ensure the measured cognitive complexity is less than 15. Let's reduce code duplication, limit the nested depth of your code, whatever it may be. Kind of these rules that will help just programmatically keep the complexity low. And then as a human developer reviewing the AI generated code, if you don't have a system that does all of this for you, it's going to be really hard for you to remember what are the key things that we want to do across my organization to have to write good code. So instead, you can leverage Sonar's quality gates, which will block your releases if the code written has too many issues. You could also use the preconfigured quality gate that Sonar has with AI code assurance as well if you don't know where to start. So up next we have this habit that says absolutely positively no stray code. Now again this is a rule that you were taught as a developer before the AI world but it's actually much more important for two reasons. One that I think about from just like my experience of doing refactors and changing things in the codebase is sometimes you're you write a new function, you move where it is in your repository and it has some logic that's different than the old function. You're AB testing and then you turn off the old function and use the new one. However, that new function may have a similar name, right? And the nuances to the logic is slightly different. So if your AI agent picks up the old function by mistake, you as a reviewer have to notice that the import is just the wrong path and all of a sudden you release code that's using the old function. The unit tests are passing, but you didn't look very closely at the unit test. So you don't realize it's using the wrong code or the wrong function originally. So there's like that kind of just general cleanup that your developer who was with you when you did the AB testing and knows that function doesn't know should no longer be used. You wouldn't expect that from here but your AI agent may not have that context and they can pick up on it. But the second reason which is much more important is that it opens up an attack vector. So, we have seen that u malicious actors can actually start tricking your LLMs to include a seemingly benign reference that it's not used right now and so you may miss it like you don't realize it's not being triggered but it could be used with mal intent in the future and this creates a massive security hole for you and that's called a backdoor or sleeper agent injection. Now you may think okay that's fine that's low risk wouldn't actually happen but actually in the research paper that we did did we saw that 30 to 40% of all of the maintainability issues detected by sonar across all five LLMs as that range is dead code that is how much dead code is being added by the LLM whenever you use them and so this just means that you have that much more code that your developers when they review have to go through and they have to highly scrutinize this unused code to be like is this something that is a potential use case in the future so we should have this if statement or is this a bad reference that we aren't using now and could be an attack vector. So this is where your developers need to adhere to the principle of minimalism. So you need to make sure that the code present should be as succinct as possible and only contain the logic that's needed. And this is where Sonar can proactively call out the dead code for you as a maintainability issue. And that greatly reprieves the developer burden when it comes to reviewing all of the new code. Now the next habit is to analyze everything. So as I've mentioned, it's up to the human engineer to review all code and own it. And it's an overwhelming amount of code that they now have to look at. But you don't only have to look at the syntax errors. You need to look for security bugs as I just talked about with this dead code and the vulnerabilities. And that was that that truly surprised me because it's like this is one that I know is a lot of people's default to go to great. They can move really quickly when you use it. Let's move forward. And so if you have advanced security in place, then what are other things that your developers can do? Well, there's a few automations within three stages of development that can help them here. First, add an IDE extension like Sonar Cube IDE that provides your immediate feedback on bugs, vulnerabilities, security hotspots, and code smells. We want to shift left. Put this in the IDE so you can catch issues as quickly as possible. Okay, let's [clears throat] talk about testing. Because laying on my side and pulling my leg up is extra effort, right? Can I do less work and still get my treat?
